skill: Linear inequalities in one or two variables

aspects.name: Translate context to inequality
aspects.description: The student reads a real-world scenario and selects (or writes) the inequality that algebraically encodes the constraint(s). The answer is an inequality expression, not a number.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 ends with an inequality as the answer; A2 ends with a numerical max/min derived from solving an inequality. If the question asks 'which inequality represents...' it is A1; if it asks 'what is the maximum/minimum...' it is A2.

types.name: Two-variable budget/capacity setup
types.description: A context gives unit costs/weights/counts of two items and a total cap; the student picks the inequality in two variables (e.g., ax + by ≤ C).
types.core_skill_demand: Map each per-item rate to its coefficient, identify the total cap as the right-hand constant, and choose the correct inequality direction (≤ for 'at most/no more than', ≥ for 'at least').

characteristics: single ≤ or ≥ constraint
small whole-number coefficients and a single bound
no compound or system structure
easy.example_stem: Leo has $15. Each container costs $1.87 and each tape $2.40. Which inequality represents the relationship between number of containers c and rolls of tape t?

characteristics: introduces a second constraint or a 'at most three times' multiplicative relation
involves comparing two quantities (e.g., a ≤ 3b) alongside a sum cap
medium.example_stem: A city employee will plant azaleas a and boxwoods b; total ≤ 164 and a ≤ 3b. Which system of inequalities models this?

characteristics: (rare for this type) typically migrates to A1.T3 when multi-step structure is added
hard.example_stem: (no pure hard examples; harder context-setup tasks appear under A1.T3.)

must_preserve: two-variable structure ax + by [op] C
real-world context giving unit costs/weights/counts for two items
answer-form is an inequality, not a number
must_vary: the two items and their unit values
the cap and the inequality direction (≤ vs ≥)
which letter is which item (avoid always x = first noun)
common_bugs: swapping coefficients and totals (using cap as a coefficient)
flipping ≤ to ≥ when paraphrasing 'no more than'
collapsing the two-variable problem into a one-variable solve (categorical drift to A2)

types.name: Range/bound on a single variable
types.description: A context gives a minimum and maximum (or a strict bound) for a single quantity; student selects a compound inequality (a ≤ x ≤ b) or single-sided bound.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize a 'between/from–to/minimum–maximum' phrasing as a compound inequality and choose strict vs non-strict ends correctly.

characteristics: explicit numerical min and max stated in the stem
single variable, no rate conversion
answer is a direct compound inequality on the same quantity
easy.example_stem: Lowest temperature was 31°F and highest was 67°F. Which inequality represents the temperature t?

characteristics: requires multiplying the per-unit range by a fixed quantity (e.g., per-day × 365)
or composing two rates into a single bound on a derived variable
the bound may use 'more than/less than' (strict)
medium.example_stem: Elephant weighs 200 lb at birth, gains >2 but <3 lb/day during first year. Inequality for weight w 365 days after birth?

characteristics: applies a theorem/derived structural rule (e.g., triangle inequality) to produce the bound
the bound is implicit, not directly stated
hard.example_stem: Triangle has sides 6 and 12; which inequality gives possible lengths x of the third side?

must_preserve: single-variable answer form (a [op] x [op] b)
real context that fixes both endpoints (or implies them via a rule)
must_vary: domain (temperature, distance, height, weight)
endpoint values and whether endpoints are strict or non-strict
common_bugs: using ≤ when stem says 'more than' (strict)
forgetting to scale per-unit range by the multiplier (medium-difficulty regression)
writing a single-sided inequality when both bounds are given

types.name: Multi-step contextual setup
types.description: A context with multiple moving parts (rates, base fees, percentages, breakpoints) asks which inequality, when solved, would answer a posed question.
types.core_skill_demand: Build a piecewise/compound algebraic expression for total cost or earnings, then express the 'at least/at most/break-even' condition correctly.

characteristics: single algebraic combination (e.g., average of four numbers) with one inequality direction
easy.example_stem: Tom scored 85, 78, 98 on first three exams. Which inequality gives the fourth score G to achieve mean of at least 90?

characteristics: introduces a recurring/per-unit charge plus a one-time fee
requires comparing two cost expressions (savings break-even)
answer-form still inequality, not numeric
medium.example_stem: Geothermal install $25,000; baseline cost $4,334/yr vs new cost $2,712/yr. Which inequality solves for years t at which total spent on new system is less than baseline?

characteristics: ≥3 quantities/rates and an implicit geometric or counting relation (e.g., perimeter formula, consecutive odd integers)
requires defining an intermediate quantity (e.g., wait time vs walk time, fourth odd integer = x+6) before writing the inequality
hard.example_stem: In four consecutive odd integers starting with x, the product of 12 and the fourth is at most 26 less than the sum of the first and third. Which inequality represents this?

must_preserve: multi-quantity context requiring construction of a compound expression
answer is an inequality, not a number
must_vary: the domain (cost, geometry, integer-sequence, electrical)
the inequality direction and the named target variable
common_bugs: simplifying the compound expression too far (loss of structure that the answer-options preserve)
dropping a piecewise/break-even comparison and producing a single-side bound (difficulty regression to T1/T2)
swapping which side of the inequality the variable lives on (relative to answer options)

aspects.name: Solve inequality for numeric extremum in context
aspects.description: Given a contextual constraint, the student must solve to obtain a numeric maximum or minimum value (often whole-number) of a variable.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 returns a number (max/min count, hours, units); A1 returns an inequality. A clone that switches the answer-form is a categorical drift.

types.name: Min additional units to reach goal
types.description: A 'have/need' scenario: current quantity is given, goal is given; student computes the minimum additional units needed.
types.core_skill_demand: Set up goal − current = additional, recognize 'at least' as ≥ and round up if integer-constrained, report the smallest integer satisfying.

characteristics: direct subtraction goal − current
small whole numbers, no rate conversion
answer is a single integer
easy.example_stem: Geologist needs at least 67 lava samples; has 63. Minimum additional samples?

characteristics: one rate conversion (e.g., distance ÷ speed = hours)
or package-size rounding (ceiling)
answer is a small whole number after one operation
medium.example_stem: Need 50 rolls; sold in packs of 12. Minimum packs to buy?

characteristics: (none in this skill’s bank — harder solves migrate to A2.T2 with multi-rate structure)

must_preserve: two-quantity 'have/need' structure with explicit threshold
numerical answer (integer)
must_vary: domain (samples, hours, words, miles, packages)
whether 'minimum' requires ceiling-rounding vs exact subtraction
common_bugs: answering with the threshold itself instead of the difference
forgetting to ceiling-round when integer units are required
flipping at-least vs at-most direction

types.name: Greatest/least count under budget or capacity
types.description: A budget/capacity constraint with one or more rate terms; student solves for the largest (or smallest) integer value of a count variable.
types.core_skill_demand: Form (rate × count) + fixed ≤ cap, isolate count, take floor (or ceiling), and report the integer.

characteristics: one fixed fee plus one per-unit rate
small whole-number arithmetic
single ceiling/floor step
easy.example_stem: Boat rental $60/hr + $10 safety course; spend no more than $280. Max whole hours?

characteristics: decimal per-unit rate (e.g., $10.25/attendee)
or two different per-unit rates (e.g., 25-lb crates and 62-lb crates) with one variable fixed by stem
requires one isolation step and one floor
medium.example_stem: Onetime fee $35, $10.25 per attendee, budget $200. Greatest number of attendees?

characteristics: two unknown counts with two competing constraints (e.g., budget AND minimum count) requiring algebraic solving of a system
involves percent overhead (sales tax, commission) before the cap is applied
requires identifying the binding constraint among multiple
hard.example_stem: Owner budgets $2,200; must buy ≥200 candles; small $4.90, large $11.60. Maximum large candles purchasable?

must_preserve: numerical (often integer) final answer for a max/min count
rate × count + fixed cost structure under a cap
must_vary: rates, fixed fees, and the cap
domain (party, shipping, retail, transit)
which variable is being maximized or minimized
common_bugs: rounding the wrong way (ceiling instead of floor) when seeking a max
treating both unknowns as free when one is constrained by another inequality (drops the system structure)
forgetting percent overhead (sales tax/commission) before applying the cap (difficulty regression)

aspects.name: Test ordered pair(s) against inequality or system
aspects.description: Given one or more inequalities, the student determines which ordered pair (or table of pairs) satisfies all of them.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A3 keeps the inequality(ies) fixed and varies the candidate points; A4 keeps a point/region fixed and asks which inequality matches a graph; A1 returns an inequality, not a point.

types.name: Point satisfies inequality or system
types.description: Given an inequality (or system), pick which ordered pair from four options satisfies it.
types.core_skill_demand: Substitute each candidate (x, y) into every inequality and verify all are simultaneously satisfied.

characteristics: single inequality with small integer coefficients
candidate points have small magnitudes
could-be answer set is finite and short
easy.example_stem: Bakery sells trays of 50–60 cookies. Which could be the total on 4 trays?

characteristics: a system of two inequalities, both must hold
moderate coefficients and at least one negative slope
decoys satisfy exactly one of the two inequalities
medium.example_stem: Which ordered pair satisfies the system y > 14 and 4x + y < 18?

characteristics: system with a partially specified point (one coordinate is symbolic) or asks about a region of valid y-coordinates
answer requires both elimination of values that fail one inequality and verification on the other
hard.example_stem: Point (x, 53) satisfies y > 14 and 4x + y < 18 — which x is valid?

must_preserve: candidate pairs as the answer form (or x/y range for partially-specified points)
the requirement to satisfy every listed inequality simultaneously
must_vary: the specific inequalities and the four candidate pairs
strict vs non-strict boundaries (one of which often is the discriminator)
common_bugs: checking only one inequality of a system (false positive on partial match)
treating strict ‘<’ as ‘≤’ and including a boundary point
swapping the substituted coordinates (x for y)

types.name: Table of (x, y) values all satisfy
types.description: Given a single inequality (e.g., y > 7x − 4), four small tables of (x, y) pairs are offered; pick the table whose every row satisfies the inequality.
types.core_skill_demand: Substitute each row of each table and verify all rows satisfy the inequality for the correct table.

characteristics: (no easy in this bank; entry-level skips this format)
single inequality with small positive slope (e.g., y > 7x − 4 or y > 4x + 8)
all candidate rows use small integers
two rows per table
medium.example_stem: For y > 7x − 4, which table has all (x, y) as solutions?

characteristics: larger coefficients (e.g., 13x or 2x − y > 883)
three rows per table or a system with two inequalities (e.g., y < x and x < 22)
decoys satisfy most but not all rows
hard.example_stem: For 2x − y > 883, which table has all rows satisfying the inequality?

must_preserve: table-of-points answer format
every row of the correct table must satisfy the inequality (or system)
must_vary: the inequality coefficients and the table values
whether the inequality is single or a 2-inequality system
common_bugs: checking only one row per table
off-by-one boundary (using ≤ where stem says <)
constructing a table whose 'correct' row only partially satisfies (silent invalidation of the clone)

aspects.name: Match graph to inequality
aspects.description: A shaded half-plane (sometimes bounded by a horizontal/vertical or slanted line, dashed or solid) is shown; student selects the inequality whose solution set matches the shaded region.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A4 requires reading a graph image; A3 requires substitution into an algebraic inequality. A clone that drops the graph (or replaces it with text-only) crosses into A3 — categorical drift.

types.name: Identify inequality from shaded half-plane
types.description: Read the boundary line (slope, intercept, horizontal y = b, or vertical x = a), determine dashed vs solid, and pick ≤, <, ≥, or > based on the shaded side.
types.core_skill_demand: Recover the boundary equation from the graph, then convert to inequality using the dashed/solid distinction and the shaded side relative to the line.

characteristics: horizontal or vertical boundary (y = constant or x = constant)
solid boundary, integer intercept
easy.example_stem: Shaded region above the horizontal line y = 12 (solid). Which inequality is represented?

characteristics: slanted boundary with integer slope and integer y-intercept
dashed vs solid distinction is the discriminator among options
medium.example_stem: Boundary y = mx + b with shaded region above (solid). Which inequality?

characteristics: non-standard axis scaling (one axis ranges much wider than the other) or negative intercept that makes slope reading harder
answer choices share the same boundary but differ in inequality direction or strict/non-strict
hard.example_stem: Boundary horizontal at y = −4 (solid); region shaded below. Which inequality?

must_preserve: graph-based stem (a shaded half-plane image)
answer is an inequality (not a point)
boundary line clearly readable from the graph
must_vary: slope and intercept values
horizontal vs vertical vs slanted boundary
dashed vs solid; shaded above vs below
common_bugs: confusing dashed (strict) with solid (non-strict)
flipping the shaded side and producing the opposite inequality
replacing the graph with text and accidentally creating an A3 question (categorical drift)

aspects.name: Algebraic interpretation/bound
aspects.description: The stem gives an algebraic relationship between two variables (often as a word-encoded phrase like 'x is at most k less than m times y') and asks for the resulting inequality or for a numerical bound on one variable given the other.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A5 has no contextual story (no money, distances, or items) — it is a pure algebraic translation/manipulation. A1.T3 also translates words to inequalities, but with a real-world context that drives the structure.

types.name: Word-encoded algebraic bound
types.description: Translate phrases like 'x is at most 17 less than 5 times y' into an inequality, then either select the inequality form or compute a numeric bound on x given a specific y.
types.core_skill_demand: Convert verbal modifiers ('at most', 'less than', 'times') into the correct algebraic inequality — most often x ≤ my − k or x ≥ my − k — and (in some variants) substitute a given y to bound x.

characteristics: (no easy items — this verbal-algebraic translation does not appear at the easy tier)
stem returns an inequality form (no substitution required)
small integer coefficients and one phrase modifier
medium.example_stem: Minimum value of x is 12 less than 6 times n. Which inequality shows possible x?

characteristics: requires substituting a specific value of one variable and computing the numeric bound on the other
answer is a single number (often an integer) on the inequality’s tight side
hard.example_stem: x is at most 2 less than 3 times y. If y = 9, what is the greatest possible value of x?

must_preserve: context-free, purely algebraic phrasing
the specific 'less/more than' + 'times' phrase pattern
must_vary: coefficients k and m, and the variable names
whether the answer is an inequality (medium) or a number (hard)
common_bugs: treating 'k less than m times y' as my + k instead of my − k
flipping 'at most' to '≥'
adding a contextual story (categorical drift into A1.T3)

types.name: Solve/interpret given inequality (no context)
types.description: A given inequality (or system) is presented and the student is asked which equivalent inequality, or which range of one coordinate, results.
types.core_skill_demand: Apply equivalent-inequality moves (add/subtract, divide by positive) or eliminate one variable from a system to derive a range on the remaining variable.

characteristics: (no easy examples in this bank.)
single inequality with one rearrangement step
involves substituting a specific value of one variable in a formula relation
medium.example_stem: Karvonen formula relating H and p; for p in a given range, which inequality describes H?

characteristics: system requires eliminating one variable to find the resolved range on the other
or requires interpreting an inequality with a discrete-count target (greatest possible count) under multiple constraints
hard.example_stem: Given 11x + 14y ≤ 115 with x ≥ 1 and y ≥ 1 (and other constraints), what is the greatest possible number of large pizzas y?

must_preserve: an algebraic (no rich-context) inequality or system as the starting point
the answer is either an equivalent inequality or a numeric extremum
must_vary: coefficients and constants
whether the question asks for an equivalent inequality vs a numeric value
common_bugs: dividing by a negative without flipping the inequality (introduces a wrong-direction decoy as the 'correct' answer)
dropping one constraint from the system and producing a looser, incorrect range
adding a context story (categorical drift into A1.T3 or A2.T2)

skill_wide_notes: Hard items concentrate in A2.T2 (multi-constraint count maximization with percent/decimal coefficients), A3.T2 (table-of-points with larger coefficients or systems), and A1.T3 (multi-step setup where the binding relation is implicit). Easy items concentrate in A1.T1, A1.T2, A2.T1, A3.T1, and A4.T1 with horizontal/vertical boundaries. A common drift risk: cloners turning an A1 'which inequality' item into an A2 'what is the maximum' item (or vice versa) — these are categorically different even when the underlying constraint is identical.
